categories.title: Broken Access Control
categories.description: Access control enforces policy such that users cannot act outside of their intended permissions. Failures typically lead to unauthorized information disclosure, modification, or destruction of all data or performing a business function outside the user's limits.
attack_scenarios: Accessing another user's account by modifying the URL
Elevation of privilege by acting as an admin without authentication
Metadata manipulation such as replaying JWT tokens with altered privileges
CORS misconfiguration allowing unauthorized API access
Force browsing to authenticated pages as unauthenticated user
how_to_test: Test every endpoint with different user roles
Check horizontal privilege escalation (user A accessing user B's data)
Check vertical privilege escalation (regular user accessing admin functions)
Test IDOR vulnerabilities in direct object references
Review CORS policies for overly permissive configurations
Test JWT token manipulation
categories.remediation: Deny by default. Enforce access control on the server side. Log access control failures and alert admins. Rate limit API and controller access. Invalidate JWT tokens on logout.

categories.title: Cryptographic Failures
categories.description: Failures related to cryptography which often leads to sensitive data exposure. Data in transit and at rest must be protected with strong cryptographic controls.
attack_scenarios: Sensitive data transmitted over unencrypted connections (HTTP)
Weak encryption algorithms (MD5, SHA1, DES)
Hard-coded cryptographic keys
Missing encryption for sensitive data at rest
Improper key management
how_to_test: Check if sensitive data is transmitted over HTTP instead of HTTPS
Test for weak SSL/TLS configurations using sslyze or testssl.sh
Search for hard-coded credentials and keys in source code
Check password storage mechanisms (bcrypt vs MD5)
Verify certificate validation is enforced
categories.remediation: Encrypt all data in transit with TLS 1.2+. Use strong hashing algorithms (bcrypt, scrypt, Argon2). Do not use deprecated crypto functions. Implement proper key management.

categories.title: Injection
categories.description: Injection flaws occur when untrusted data is sent to an interpreter as part of a command or query. Attacker's hostile data can trick the interpreter into executing unintended commands or accessing data without proper authorization.
attack_scenarios: SQL injection via user input fields
OS command injection through file upload features
LDAP injection in authentication systems
XPath injection in XML-based systems
SSTI (Server-Side Template Injection)
how_to_test: Test all input fields for SQL injection using sqlmap
Test for XSS (reflected, stored, DOM) using dalfox
Test file upload endpoints for command injection
Check LDAP queries for injection possibilities
Test template rendering endpoints for SSTI
categories.remediation: Use parameterized queries or prepared statements. Use ORM frameworks. Validate and sanitize all user inputs. Implement allowlists. Use LIMIT clauses in SQL queries.

categories.title: Insecure Design
categories.description: Insecure design is a broad category representing different weaknesses expressed as 'missing or ineffective control design.' Focuses on risks related to design and architectural flaws.
attack_scenarios: Missing rate limiting on credential recovery flows
Business logic flaws allowing price manipulation
Insecure direct object references by design
how_to_test: Review application architecture and data flow diagrams
Test business logic for abuse cases
Check for missing rate limiting on sensitive operations
Test multi-step workflows for state manipulation
categories.remediation: Establish a secure development lifecycle. Use threat modeling. Integrate security design patterns. Implement unit and integration tests for security controls.

categories.title: Security Misconfiguration
categories.description: Security misconfiguration is the most commonly seen issue. Without a concerted, repeatable application security configuration process, systems are at higher risk.
attack_scenarios: Default admin credentials left unchanged
Directory listing enabled on web server
Verbose error messages exposing stack traces
Unnecessary features enabled (ports, services, pages)
Missing security hardening across the stack
how_to_test: Check for default credentials on all services
Test for directory listing and path disclosure
Review HTTP security headers
Check for exposed admin interfaces
Scan for unnecessary open ports and services
categories.remediation: Implement a repeatable hardening process. Remove or disable unused features. Review and update configurations as part of patch management. Segment application architecture.

categories.title: Vulnerable and Outdated Components
categories.description: Components such as libraries, frameworks, and other software modules run with the same privileges as the application. If a vulnerable component is exploited, such an attack can facilitate data loss or server takeover.
attack_scenarios: Exploiting known CVEs in outdated web frameworks
Using publicly available exploits for unpatched components
Vulnerable JavaScript libraries in frontend applications
how_to_test: Scan for known CVEs using nuclei CVE templates
Check component versions and compare against NVD/CVE databases
Use dependency scanning tools (npm audit, pip audit)
Review server software versions via HTTP headers
categories.remediation: Continuously inventory component versions. Monitor CVE databases. Subscribe to security advisories. Implement automated dependency updates. Only use components from official sources.

categories.title: Identification and Authentication Failures
categories.description: Confirmation of the user's identity, authentication, and session management is critical to protect against authentication-related attacks.
attack_scenarios: Credential stuffing attacks using breached credentials
Brute force attacks without lockout mechanisms
Weak or default passwords
Insecure session token implementation
Missing MFA on sensitive actions
how_to_test: Test for account lockout mechanisms
Check password complexity requirements
Test session fixation and session hijacking
Verify secure session cookie flags (HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite)
Test password reset flows for weaknesses
categories.remediation: Implement MFA. Do not deploy with default credentials. Use bcrypt for passwords. Implement weak password checks. Use secure session management. Limit failed login attempts.

categories.title: Software and Data Integrity Failures
categories.description: Software and data integrity failures relate to code and infrastructure that does not protect against integrity violations. An example is where an application relies upon plugins, libraries, or modules from untrusted sources, repositories, and content delivery networks.
attack_scenarios: SolarWinds-style supply chain attack
Insecure deserialization leading to RCE
Auto-update without integrity verification
Using CDN resources without Subresource Integrity (SRI)
how_to_test: Check CI/CD pipelines for unauthorized access
Test deserialization endpoints for gadget chains
Verify SRI hashes on CDN-hosted resources
Review auto-update mechanisms for integrity checks
categories.remediation: Use digital signatures for code. Only consume libraries from trusted repos with verified hashes. Ensure CI/CD pipelines have integrity controls. Implement deserialization protection.

categories.title: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
categories.description: This category helps detect, escalate, and respond to active breaches. Without logging and monitoring, breaches cannot be detected. Insufficient logging and monitoring allows attackers to achieve their goals without detection.
attack_scenarios: Audit trails missing for login failures
Logs not monitored for suspicious patterns
Logs stored locally and cleared by attacker
Missing alerting for high-value transactions
how_to_test: Verify that login failures generate log entries
Test that high-value transactions are logged
Check if logs include sufficient detail for forensic reconstruction
Verify logs are protected from tampering
Test penetration activities and verify they appear in logs
categories.remediation: Ensure all authentication, access control, and server-side input validation failures are logged with sufficient context. Ensure logs are in a format easily consumed by SIEM. Establish incident response and recovery plans.

categories.title: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
categories.description: SSRF flaws occur whenever a web application is fetching a remote resource without validating the user-supplied URL. Allows an attacker to coerce the application to send a crafted request to an unexpected destination.
attack_scenarios: Accessing cloud metadata endpoints (169.254.169.254)
Port scanning internal networks via SSRF
Accessing internal services not exposed to internet
Exfiltrating data via DNS-based SSRF
how_to_test: Find URL/endpoint parameters and test with internal addresses
Test with cloud metadata endpoints (AWS, GCP, Azure)
Use OAST (out-of-band) techniques to detect blind SSRF
Test protocol smuggling (file://, dict://, gopher://)
Check redirect handling for open redirects leading to SSRF
categories.remediation: Sanitize and validate all client-supplied input data. Enforce URL schema, port, and destination with an allowlist. Do not send raw responses to clients. Disable HTTP redirections. Use network segmentation.
